coredata.dc:title: Plasma-assisted mechanochemistry to produce polyamide/boron nitride nanocomposites with high thermal conductivities and mechanical properties
coredata.dc:creator: You, Jiwan
Choi, Han-Hyeong
Lee, Young Mo
Cho, Jaehyun
Park, Min
Lee, Sang-Soo
Park, Jong Hyuk
coredata.dc:description: Polymer nanocomposites incorporating nanofillers have achieved a variety of functionalities including mechanical, chemical, thermal, and electrical properties. However, the fundamental problem in such composites, poor compatibility between polymers and nanofillers, has limited the development of functionalities. Herein, we demonstrate a simple and effective approach to address this issue without using compatibilizers. The plasma-assisted mechanochemistry (PMC) process can readily form covalent bonds between polymers and nanofillers even in the solid state, providing excellent processability; it is also cost effective and environmentally friendly. Polyamide 66 (PA66) and hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) were compounded via the PMC process, which enhanced the interfacial affinity between PA66 and h-BN and promoted uniform dispersion of h-BN platelets in the composites. The resulting PA66/h-BN nanocomposites exhibited significantly improved mechanical properties and thermal conductivities. In particular, the degradation in tensile strength of the composites at high h-BN content was completely prevented by the PMC process and the thermal conductivities of the composites were over four times higher than those of conventional composites. Therefore, this approach can produce polymer nanocomposites with improved functionalities, thus greatly extending their applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer-matrix composites
Thermoplastic resin
Mechanical properties
Interface/interphase
Thermal properties

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Formation of covalent bonds between PA66 and h-BN

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic and energy diagrams for the plasma-assisted mechanochemistry (PMC) process. Mechanical force was applied to the PA66/h-BN mixture by rotating the blade at 2500 rpm for 15 min. During the process, the mixture was simultaneously treated with nitrogen plasma of 1.5 kV. Covalent bonds between PA66 and h-BN formed due to the reduction of the activation energy (Ea) by the mechanical forces and by the plasma treatment. (b) Schematic diagram for preparing the PA66/h-BN composites. The PMC-processed particles were melt-blended and then injection-molded (the PMCMI process).

FT-IR spectra of the PA66/h-BN composites in the wavelength range of (a) 3500–750 cm−1 and (b) 1700–750 cm−1. The composites contained 5 wt% h-BN and were prepared by the PMCMI, MCMI, and MI processes. The spectra were calibrated using the C=O peak at 1630 cm−1 as an internal standard for quantitative analyses. The FT-IR spectra of pristine PA66 and h-BN are also presented for comparison. The PMCMI composite spectrum shows characteristic peaks at 1078 and 1100 cm−1, corresponding to C–N and C–B bonds, respectively. In addition, the h-BN peaks at 1360 and 810 cm−1 of the PMCMI composite are much more intense than those of the MCMI and MI composites.

XPS spectra of the PA66/h-BN composites for the (a) C1s, (b) N1s, (c) B1s, and (d) O1s regions. The composites contained 5 wt% h-BN and were prepared by the PMCMI, MCMI, and MI processes. Each curve was deconvoluted into multiple peaks that revealed the different chemical states. The peaks observed in the MI composite are indicated by dashed lines.

Proposed mechanisms of the grafting reaction between PA66 and h-BN during the PMC process. The structure of PA66 can be changed by radical generation and chain scission, resulting in the formation of covalent bonds between PA66 and h-BN.

Micro X-ray CT images of the PA66/h-BN composites and size distribution of the h-BN platelets dispersed in the composites. The composites contained 10 wt% h-BN and were prepared by the (a,b) PMCMI, (c,d) MCMI, and (e,f) MI processes. The size of the h-BN platelets was statistically calculated from the micro-CT images. The observed dimensions were approximately 1 × 1 × 1 mm3.

Thermal analyses of the PA66/h-BN composites. The composites contained 5 wt% h-BN and were prepared by the PMCMI, MCMI, and MI processes. (a) DSC and (b) TGA curves for the composites. The orange dotted line in panel (b) corresponds to 95 wt% and the intersection of the line and the TGA curve indicates the thermal decomposition temperature of each composite. The inset in panel (b) shows the first derivative of the TGA curves. (c) Storage modulus and (d) tan δ curves as a function of temperature for the composites as measured using DMA. The properties of neat PA66 are also presented for comparison. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Mechanical properties of the PA66/h-BN composites. The composites were prepared by the PMCMI, MCMI, and MI processes and the h-BN content was controlled at 1, 5, 10, and 20 wt%. (a) Stress–strain curves, (b) tensile strength, (c) Young's modulus, and (d) elongation at break for the PA66/h-BN composites. The sample code for the composites in panel (a) indicates the fabrication process and h-BN content. The mechanical properties of neat PA66 are presented for comparison.

Thermal conductivity of the PA66/h-BN composites. The composites were prepared by the PMCMI, MCMI, and MI processes and the h-BN content was controlled at 1, 5, 10, and 20 wt%. All specimens had dimensions of 50 × 50 × 10 mm3. (a) Through-plane and (b) in-plane thermal conductivities of the PA66/h-BN composites at room temperature. Neat PA66 had a thermal conductivity of 0.25 W/mK in both directions. Comparative study of the thermal conductivities of polymer/BN composites in the (c) through-plane and (d) in-plane directions. The numbers in the legend indicate the reference numbers. The abbreviations are as follows: boron nitride nanotubes (BNNT), boron nitride nanosheets (BNNS), poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA), polyimide (PI), poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS), poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF), thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU), and poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA). The PA66/GNP composites prepared via the PMCMI process exhibited significantly improved thermal conductivity compared to the other indicated composites.

Heat transfer and dissipation properties of the PA66/h-BN composites. The composites contained 10 wt% h-BN and were prepared by the PMCMI, MCMI, and MI processes. Thermal images of the PMCMI, MCMI, and MI (from left to right) composites during (a) heating and (b) cooling. The thermal image of neat PA66 (rightmost) is presented for comparison. All specimens had dimensions of 12 × 62 × 3 mm3. For heating, the specimens were placed on a hot plate at a constant temperature of 120 °C. For cooling, the specimens were preheated in an oven at 120 °C and then exposed to room temperature. Time–temperature profiles of the specimens during (c) heating and (d) cooling are shown.

head.ce:title: Plasma-assisted mechanochemistry to produce polyamide/boron nitride nanocomposites with high thermal conductivities and mechanical properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: KU-KIST Graduate School of Converging Science and Technology, Korea University, Seoul, 02841, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Photo-Electronic Hybrids Research Center, Korea Institute of Science and Technology, Seoul, 02792, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Seoul National University, Seoul, 08826, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Korea University, Seoul, 02841, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Composite Materials Application Research Center, Korea Institute of Science and Technology, Jeollabuk-do, 55324, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Korea Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: KHU-KIST Department of Converging Science and Technology, Kyung Hee University, Seoul, 02447, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: KHU-KIST Department of Converging Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymer nanocomposites incorporating nanofillers have achieved a variety of functionalities including mechanical, chemical, thermal, and electrical properties. However, the fundamental problem in such composites, poor compatibility between polymers and nanofillers, has limited the development of functionalities. Herein, we demonstrate a simple and effective approach to address this issue without using compatibilizers. The plasma-assisted mechanochemistry (PMC) process can readily form covalent bonds between polymers and nanofillers even in the solid state, providing excellent processability; it is also cost effective and environmentally friendly. Polyamide 66 (PA66) and hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) were compounded via the PMC process, which enhanced the interfacial affinity between PA66 and h-BN and promoted uniform dispersion of h-BN platelets in the composites. The resulting PA66/h-BN nanocomposites exhibited significantly improved mechanical properties and thermal conductivities. In particular, the degradation in tensile strength of the composites at high h-BN content was completely prevented by the PMC process and the thermal conductivities of the composites were over four times higher than those of conventional composites. Therefore, this approach can produce polymer nanocomposites with improved functionalities, thus greatly extending their applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Composites refer to a mixture of two or more constituent materials with different physical and chemical properties. They can be imparted with functionalities that cannot be achieved by single materials [1–5]. As a representative example, the inherent properties of pure polymers including their mechanical, chemical, thermal, and electrical properties can be significantly improved by compounding them with various fillers. However, in general, most polymers and fillers are poorly compatible with each other due to their low affinity, which deteriorates the properties of the polymer composites. Although numerous compounding techniques for polymer composites have been developed to address this issue, many would still benefit from more advanced approaches. For example, melt-blending, one of the most commonly used processes in industry, cannot provide strong affinity between polymers and fillers. Hence, melt-blended fillers with nanoscale dimensions are often aggregated within polymer composites [6,7]. In addition, wet chemistry methods using solvents are less preferred in industry due to reduced processability, high production cost, and environmental pollution issues [8–10]. Given these circumstances, it is still necessary to develop a simple, low-cost, and high-throughput compounding method that can overcome these problems.

Mechanochemistry methods that induce chemical reactions by applying mechanical energy can greatly enhance the affinity between different types of materials [11–15]. Mechanochemistry techniques are carried out under dry conditions without solvents, making them relatively easy processes, cost effective, and environmentally friendly. Moreover, such fabrication methods are highly suitable for polymer composites because they are compatible with conventional compounding processes and can be used with very small fillers including nanomaterials [16–18]. Recently developed mechanochemistry processes coupled with plasma treatments can form covalent bonds between chemically less active materials, even in the solid state [19]. This combination is termed the plasma-assisted mechanochemistry (PMC) process. Compounding using the mechanochemistry technique can be an effective solution to the fundamental problem facing the preparation of polymer nanocomposites, i.e., poor compatibility between polymers and nanofillers.

Hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) is a promising filler for functional polymer nanocomposites. Like graphite, h-BN has a two-dimensional layered structure with excellent mechanical strength and thermal conductivity [20–23]. In addition, it is thermally and chemically stable because B and N atoms are alternatively connected via strong covalent bonds. On the other hand, it exhibits characteristics distinct from graphite, such as electrical insulating properties and colorless appearance, due to its large band gap. Despite these particular properties, conventional polymer composites containing h-BN are limited in developing such functionalities because h-BN has low affinity with polymers, which causes aggregation in the polymer composites [24,25]. Many approaches have been proposed to enhance the affinity between h-BN and polymers in polymer composites. The thermal treatment and radical reaction of h-BN in solvents for compounding with polymers have been reported [26]. In addition, the compatibility of h-BN with polymers has been improved by covalent or non-covalent functionalization [27,28]. However, these methods using wet chemistry are less preferred because of their low productivity and high production cost. Hence, the formation of covalent bonds between h-BN and polymers via mechanochemistry methods can be a suitable route to achieve the homogeneous dispersion of h-BN and to enhance the functionalities of the resulting polymer composites.

Herein, we introduce an effective compounding method for the production of polymer nanocomposites under dry conditions via the PMC process. Polyamide 66 (PA66) and h-BN were chosen as the base polymer and filler for the composites, respectively. The mechanochemistry technique successfully formed covalent bonds between PA66 and h-BN in the absence of any binders, giving high interfacial affinity. The resulting PA66/h-BN composites exhibited significantly enhanced mechanical properties and thermal conductivities due to the uniform dispersion of h-BN platelets without aggregation. This approach can be applied to a variety of polymers and nanofillers, which will greatly aid the production of polymer nanocomposites with improved functionalities.

PA66 (24A grade, LG Chem) pellets were ground to powders with an average particle size of ca. 400 μm. The density and melt flow index of the PA66 were 1.14 g cm−3 and 45.6 g (10 min−1) (275 °C, 2.16 kg), respectively. The h-BN (Platelets 012, 3M) platelets had an average diameter of ca. 12 μm and an average thickness of ca. 500 nm. The PA66 and h-BN powders were stored in an oven at 60 °C to prevent water absorption.

The equipment for the PMC process (Nanocular system, Hosokawa Micron) included a reaction chamber with a rotor that applied strong shear, compressive, and frictional forces to the mixed materials [19]. The PA66 and h-BN powders were placed in the chamber and the h-BN content in the mixture was adjusted to 1, 5, 10, and 20 wt%. The chamber was thoroughly purged with nitrogen and then the pressure was maintained at 102–103 Pa. To form covalent bonds between the mixed materials, the rotor was rotated at 2500 rpm for 15 min while nitrogen plasma was generated at 1.5 kV. During the process, the chamber was cooled by circulating water to keep the temperature constant.

The PA66/h-BN mixtures treated with the PMC process were melt-blended using a twin-screw extruder (PRISM TSE 16 TC, Thermo Electron). The melt blending was carried out at a screw speed of 100 rpm over the temperature range of 260–265 °C. The extruded material was molded into specimens using an injection molding machine (BOY12A, BOY Machines). The injection molding was performed at a temperature range of 260–265 °C and a screw speed of 200 rpm, with the injection and back pressures set at 150 and 8.5 bar, respectively. We call this entire process, consisting of the PMC process, melt blending, and injection molding, the PMCMI process, and the resulting product the PMCMI composite. Two control samples were prepared for comparison. First, PA66/h-BN mixtures were subjected to the mechanochemistry process without plasma treatment (the MC process), followed by melt blending and injection molding. This process was named the MCMI process and provided MCMI composites. Second, PA66/h-BN composites were prepared by the MI process consisting of melt blending and injection molding and denoted by the MI composites.

A Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Nicolet 380, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.) with attenuated total reflection accessories was used to study the chemical structure of the PA66/h-BN composites. The chemical states of the C, N, B and O elements in the composites were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (K-Alpha+, Thermo Fisher Scientific). The morphology and dispersion state of the h-BN platelets in the composites were observed by X-ray micro-computed tomography (micro-CT) (SkyScan 1172, Bruker) [29,30]. The detailed conditions for the micro-CT measurements were as follows. X-ray source voltage and current: 33 kV and 204 μA, respectively; monitored sample size: ca. 1 × 1 × 1 mm3; projection numbers per sample: 1800; exposure time per projection: 1430 ms. Micro-CT images of the composites were obtained by reconstructing the projections. The size distribution of the h-BN platelets in the composites was investigated using software (CTVox and CTAnalyser) for three-dimensional modeling and volume analyses. The thermal properties of the composites were measured using differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) (DSC 7, PerkinElmer), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (Q50, TA Instruments), and dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) (Q 800, TA Instruments). In the thermal analyses, the temperature was varied at a heating or cooling rate of 10 °C min−1. The DSC curves for the second heating were used to investigate the melting behavior of the PA66/h-BN composites. TGA and DMA measurements were performed under a nitrogen atmosphere. A universal testing machine (5567, Instron) was used to measure the mechanical properties of the composites according to the ASTM D638. Tensile tests were carried out using type V specimens at a crosshead speed of 5 mm min−1 at ca. 22 °C and 20% relative humidity. The thermal conductivity of the composites was quantified using a thermal constants analyzer (TPS 2500 S, Hot Disk AB) based on the hot disk transient plane source method. The through-plane and in-plane thermal conductivities were measured at room temperature using specimens with dimensions of 50 × 50 × 10 mm3. The heat transfer and dissipation properties were characterized using an infrared camera (E40, FLIR Systems) as follows. Composite specimens with dimensions of 12 × 62 × 3 mm3 were prepared. The transient temperature response during heating was observed by heating the specimens on a hot plate at 120 °C and then recording the thermal images for 500 s. To evaluate the transient temperature response during cooling, specimens preheated in an oven at 120 °C were placed on paper at room temperature and their thermal images over time were recorded for 300 s.

To form covalent bonds between polymers and nanofillers in polymer nanocomposites, sufficient energy must be supplied to the materials. Mechanochemistry techniques exert a strong mechanical force on the materials, which causes local temperature increases and generates radicals by breaking chemical bonds in the polymers [31,32]. The radicals react with the filler surfaces to induce interfacial grafting between the polymers and the nanofillers. Because simultaneous plasma treatment can promote radical generation and grafting reaction, the mechanochemistry process combined with plasma treatment, i.e., the PMC process, can greatly improve the compatibility between the polymers and the nanofillers [19].

Fig. 1 a schematically describes the formation of covalent bonds at the interfaces between PA66 and h-BN during the PMC process. PA66 and h-BN powders were mixed together in the chamber and the blade was rotated to apply shear, compressive, and frictional forces to the mixture. Additional energy was also supplied to the mixture via nitrogen plasma treatment. As shown in the energy diagram, the activation energy for the grafting reaction can be significantly reduced due to the energy provided during the PMC process [33]. The PMC process produced PA66 particles coated with h-BN platelets because the particles were much larger than the platelets. The PMC-processed particles were light yellow while the particles prepared by the MC process or by simple mixing were white (Fig. S1 in the Supplementary Information). This color change implied that a chemical reaction occurred during the PMC process [26]. The PMC-processed particles were melt-blended and then injection-molded, producing PMCMI composites (Fig. 1b). The structure and properties of the PMCMI composites were investigated and compared to those of control samples (the MCMI and MI composites).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of covalent bonds between PA66 and h-BN
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 displays the FT-IR spectra of the PA66/h-BN composites prepared via the different processes. The FT-IR spectra of neat PA66 and h-BN are also presented for comparison. The spectrum of the MI composite was almost identical to that of PA66. The absorption peak at 810 cm−1, which corresponds to the out-of-plane bending vibration of h-BN, was the only difference between the spectra of the MI composite and PA66. By contrast, the PMCMI composite showed a clearly different spectrum from the MI composite and PA66; new absorption peaks were found at 1078 (C–N bonds) and 1100 (C–B bonds) cm−1, which indicates the formation of covalent bonds between PA66 and h-BN via the PMC process [34,35]. The h-BN-related peaks at 1360 and 810 cm−1 of the PMCMI composite were much stronger than those of the MI composite. Moreover, the peak intensities significantly decreased for the N–H groups at 3300, 3080, and 1530 cm−1 and for the alkyl groups at 2930 and 2860 cm−1, presumably due to the radical generation and chain scission of PA66 that occurred during the PMC process [36,37]. The chemical structure of the MCMI composite was analyzed to investigate the effects of plasma treatment during the PMC process. As shown in Fig. 2, the FT-IR spectrum of the MCMI composite resembled that of the MI composite rather than that of the PMCMI composite, which indicates that covalent bonds between PA66 and h-BN hardly formed during the MC process. The peak intensities of the N–H and alkyl groups of the MCMI composite were lower than those of the MI composite but greater than those of the PMCMI composite. This result implies that the radical generation and chain scission of PA66 still occurred during the MC process, but to a lesser extent than in the PMC process.

Fig. 3 shows the XPS spectra of the PA66/h-BN composites prepared via the PMCMI, MCMI, and MI processes. The XPS curves were deconvoluted into multiple peaks to determine the chemical states of the C, N, B, and O elements in each composite. In the C1s spectrum (Fig. 3a), the MCMI and MI composites displayed only three peaks at 284.8, 285.8, and 287.9 eV, corresponding to the C–C, C–N, and C=O bonds, respectively [38,39]. However, the PMCMI composite exhibited an additional peak at 283.5 eV, demonstrating the formation of C–B bonds between PA66 and h-BN [40]. In addition, new peaks were observed at 400.5 (C–N bonds) and 189.0 (C–B bonds) eV in the N1s and B1s spectra of the PMCMI composite but not in the spectra of the MCMI and MI composites (Fig. 3b and c) [34,41]. These results are in good agreement with the FT-IR results shown in Fig. 2b, which indicates that covalent bonds between PA66 and h-BN were formed only during the PMC process. Quantitative analysis was performed using the C1s and B1s spectra of the PMCMI composite to evaluate the degree of bond formation between PA66 and h-BN. In the C1s spectrum, the peak area ratio of C–C bonds (at PA66) to C–B bonds (between PA66 and h-BN) was 9:1. Moreover, the peak area ratio of B–N bonds (mainly in h-BN) to B–C bonds (between PA66 and h-BN) was 4:1 in the B1s spectrum. These results indicate that a significant number of covalent bonds between PA66 and h-BN were formed through the PMC process. Interestingly, the O1s spectra of all three composites were identical to that of PA66 (Fig. 3d) [42]. Therefore, we assumed that no chemical reaction occurred with the C=O group of PA66 during the PMC process. For this reason, the FT-IR spectra shown in Fig. 2 were calibrated using the C=O peak at 1630 cm−1 as an internal standard for quantitative analyses. Compared to the MI composite, the MCMI composite exhibited one additional peak at 398.2 eV, corresponding to the –N = moiety [33]. Its formation is attributed to degradation of PA66 during the MC process. The FT-IR and XPS results indicate that the PMC process is highly effective for generating covalent bonds between PA66 and h-BN, thus greatly enhancing the affinity between them.

Fig. 4 illustrates possible reaction mechanisms for the changes in chemical structure of PA66 and h-BN that occurred during the PMC process. This process can generate radicals in PA66 through hydrogen atom removal or chain scission. The removal of hydrogen atoms in PA66 occurs mainly at C atoms adjacent to N atoms [33]. The radicals on C atoms can react with N and B atoms in h-BN, forming (I) C–N and (II) C–B bonds between PA66 and h-BN, respectively. Otherwise, the radicals are removed by termination reactions and form (V) –N = bonds. In addition to hydrogen atom removal, the PMC process can cleave PA66 chains by primarily breaking C–N bonds with relatively small bond energies. As a result, (III) and (IV) N–B and C–N bonds can be created between PA66 and h-BN due to the differences in electronegativity of C, N, and B atoms [34,41]. These proposed reaction mechanisms are consistent with the FT-IR and XPS results that indicate the formation of new C–B and C–N bonds. Based on the unchanged O1s XPS spectra shown in Fig. 3d, we assumed that the C=O groups of PA66 did not participate in the grafting reaction between PA66 and h-BN during the PMC process. Further studies will be conducted to demonstrate the detailed reaction mechanisms.

Three-dimensional (3D) X-ray micro-CT analyses can precisely investigate the internal structure of polymer–nanofiller composites without destroying the specimens [29,30]. The dispersion of h-BN platelets in each type of composite was observed by using a 3D X-ray micro-CT scanner. Fig. 5 shows micro-CT images of the composites filled with 10 wt% h-BN and the size distribution of the h-BN platelets dispersed in each composite. The observed dimensions of the composites were approximately 1 × 1 × 1 mm3. The h-BN platelets were uniformly dispersed in the PMCMI composite (Fig. 5a). Their average size was 11.2 μm (Fig. 5b), almost identical to the value (ca. 12 μm) of the technical specification, which implies that most h-BN platelets were not aggregated. By contrast, the conventional compounding process was not effective at uniformly dispersing the fillers in the composites. The MI composite exhibited poor dispersion of h-BN (Fig. 5e); a number of large h-BN platelets (over 20 μm) are evident in the images. The average size of the h-BN platelets of the MI composite was 24.9 μm (Fig. 5f), more than twice that of the PMCMI composite. Compared to the MI composite, the MCMI composite prepared by applying shear, compressive, and frictional forces had improved interfacial affinity between PA66 and h-BN. However, because the affinity in the MCMI composites was relatively weak due to the absence of the plasma treatment, the dispersion of h-BN platelets was not as good in the MCMI composites as in the PMCMI composites (Fig. 5c and d). These results demonstrate that the strong interfacial affinity between polymers and nanofillers provided by the PMCMI process promoted the uniform dispersion of h-BN platelets in the composites.

The PA66/h-BN composites were also characterized by thermal analyses. Fig. 6 a shows the DSC curves of each type of composite containing 5 wt% h-BN. The PMCMI composite had a melting point of 278.6 °C, which was significantly higher than those of the other composites (MCMI: 266.7 °C and MI: 265.3 °C) and neat PA66 (265.5 °C). It is probably because the interfacial affinity between PA66 and h-BN was enhanced by the formation of covalent bonds in the PMCMI composite, making the crystalline phase in PA66 solid and stable [43]. The TGA curves of the same composites are presented in Fig. 6b. The intersection of the TGA curves and the orange dotted line at 95 wt% indicates the thermal decomposition temperature of each composite. Although the three composites had the same composition of 95 wt% PA66 and 5 wt% h-BN, their TGA curves were considerably different. The PMCMI composite began to thermally decompose at a lower temperature (363.5 °C) than PA66 (381.7 °C) or the other composites (MCMI: 386.8 °C and MI: 395.4 °C). It may have resulted from a decrease in the molecular weight of PA66 caused by chain scission that occurred during the PMCMI process (Fig. 4). Interestingly, the first derivative of the TGA curve of the PMCMI composite revealed two peaks (around 415 and 450 °C) while the other composites and neat PA66 showed only one peak. It is possible that the network of new covalent bonds (C–B and N–B) formed by the PMCMI process had higher bond energies compared to normal bonds (C–C and C–N) in PA66 [38], which led to a higher thermal decomposition temperature. Moreover, the weight retention of the PMCMI, MCMI, and MI composites at 700 °C was 9.7%, 7.8%, and 6.6%, respectively. The residual amount of PA66 at high temperatures increased with the formation of covalent bonds during the PMCMI process [19,38]. The viscoelastic behaviors of the PA66/h-BN composites were studied by measuring their storage modulus and tan δ as a function of temperature using DMA (Fig. 6c and d). At all temperatures, the storage modulus of the PMCMI composite was much larger than that of the other composites. In addition, the glass transition temperature of the PMCMI, MCMI, and MI composites, determined as the peak position of the tan δ curve, was 86.7, 76.6, and 68.5 °C, respectively. These results indicate that the PMCMI process, which offers a strong interfacial affinity between polymers and nanofillers and the uniform dispersion of nanofillers, is highly effective for the production of stiff and rigid composites.

PA66/h-BN composites with various h-BN contents were prepared by each process and their mechanical properties were investigated. Fig. 7 a shows the stress–strain curves of the various composites as a function of the h-BN content. The tensile strength, Young's modulus, and elongation at break for the composites with h-BN contents of 1, 5, 10, and 20 wt% were obtained from the stress–strain curves (Fig. 7b–d). The Young's modulus increased but the tensile strength and elongation at break decreased with increasing h-BN content. Surprisingly, the PMCMI composite exhibited improved tensile strength, Young's modulus, and elongation at break at all h-BN contents compared to the MCMI and MI composites with the same h-BN content. These excellent mechanical properties were attributed to the formation of covalent bonds between PA66 and h-BN through the PMCMI process, providing a strong interfacial affinity and the uniform dispersion of h-BN platelets in the composite. Moreover, the degradation of tensile strength at high h-BN content could be completely prevented by the PMCMI process. For example, the tensile strength of the PMCMI composite filled with even 20 wt% h-BN (82.3 MPa) was greater than that of neat PA66 (79.2 MPa) and the MCMI (81.9 MPa) and MI (75.1 MPa) composites filled with 1 wt% h-BN. Although increasing the h-BN content inevitably decreased the elongation at break of all of the composites, the degree of reduction was lowered through the PMCMI process; the elongation at break of the PMCMI composites was at least 30% greater than that of the MI composites at all h-BN contents. In addition, the fracture surfaces of the PMCMI composite filled with 20 wt% h-BN were still ductile, whereas those of the MI composite exhibited a brittle morphology (Fig. S2 in the Supplementary Information). These results confirmed that the PMCMI process enabled the formation of covalent bonds between PA66 and h-BN at the h-BN content of 20 wt% and provided enhanced mechanical properties for the composites.

It is established that h-BN with its high thermal conductivities (30 and 600 W/mK for the though-plane and in-plane directions, respectively) is one of the most effective fillers to enhance the heat transfer and dissipation properties of polymer composites [20,21,44,45]. The PMCMI process offers two key benefits: (i) uniform dispersion of h-BN platelets to form a continuous h-BN network and (ii) strong interfacial affinity between PA66 and h-BN to mitigate unwanted phonon scattering at the interfaces. Hence, the PMCMI composites are expected to reduce thermal resistance and have greatly improved thermal conductivities as well as heat transfer and dissipation properties.

Fig. 8 a shows the through-plane thermal conductivities of each type of composite as a function of the h-BN content. As reported in previous studies, the thermal conductivity increased with increasing h-BN content. However, the degree of increase in the thermal conductivity was highly dependent on the preparation process of the composites. For example, PMCMI composites filled with 1, 5, 10, and 20 wt% h-BN had through-plane thermal conductivities of 1.34, 2.15, 3.02, and 3.68 W/mK, respectively. These values were significantly larger than the thermal conductivities of the MI composites with the same h-BN content (0.43, 0.54, 0.61, and 0.68 W/mK, respectively). The in-plane thermal conductivities of the composites according to the h-BN content were given in Fig. 8b; the trend was similar to that of the through-plane thermal conductivities. When the h-BN content increased from 1 to 20 wt%, the in-plane thermal conductivities of the PMCMI composites increased from 9.10 to 26.13 W/mK. Surprisingly, these values were over four times higher than that of the MI composites with the same h-BN content. For the MCMI composites, the thermal conductivities were higher than those of the MI composites but lower than those of the PMCMI composites. It was attributed to an intermediate interfacial affinity between PA66 and h-BN in the MCMI composites, which was stronger than that in the MI composites but weaker than that in the PMCMI composites, as discussed above.

A comparative study of the thermal conductivity of polymer/BN composites was conducted to quantify the role of the PMCMI process in improving the functionalities. Previous studies have reported that high BN levels lead to high thermal conductivity. However, the BN content was limited to 20 wt% or less in our study because higher levels led to poorer mechanical properties and processability. For comparison, polymer/BN composites with high thermal conductivities were selected from recent studies (Fig. 8c and d). Interestingly, both through-plane and in-plane thermal conductivities of the PMCMI composites far exceeded those of most of the selected polymer/BN composites [46–53], which indicates the superiority of the PMCMI process over other methods.

In order to use the PA66/h-BN composites as a heat sink, the heat transfer and dissipation properties were investigated by observing the transient temperature responses during heating and cooling. Fig. 9 a provides infrared thermal images of the PA66/h-BN composites with heating time at 120 °C. The PMCMI composite showed rapid temperature changes in response to heating, but the surface temperature remained uniform at all times, implying excellent heat transfer property. In contrast, the surface temperatures of other composites and neat PA66 gradually changed with heating time and were non-uniform at different locations. To study the heat dissipation property of the composites, they were preheated at 120 °C and then cooled naturally at room temperature. The infrared thermal images during the cooling test are shown in Fig. 9b. The PMCMI composite showed a much faster temperature drop with cooling time, indicating improved heat dissipation property. These results confirmed that the high thermal conductivities achieved through the PMCMI process can lead to improved heat transfer and dissipation properties in the resulting composites. The time-temperature profiles of the composites during heating and cooling are also presented (Fig. 9c and d). In the heating test, the temperature of the PMCMI composite reached 120 °C at 210 s, which is the same as the heating temperature. In contrast, other composites and neat PA66 took longer to reach the maximum temperature and their maximum temperatures were lower than 120 °C. In the cooling test, at a cooling time of 210 s, the temperature of the PMCMI composite dropped to 46.1 °C, which was over 20 °C lower than that of other composites (MCMI: 66.8 °C, MI: 71.2 °C), and neat PA66 (76.4 °C). These results demonstrate the excellent heat transfer and dissipation properties of the PMCMI composite.

We developed a process that effectively addresses the compatibility issue between polymers and nanofillers, thus improving a variety of functionalities of polymer nanocomposites. The PMC process enables the formation of covalent bonds between PA66 and h-BN, providing strong interfacial affinity and promoting the uniform dispersion of h-BN platelets. The resulting PA66/h-BN composites had much better thermal conductivities and mechanical properties than conventional composites. Moreover, the PMC process is simple, solvent-free, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly. Our findings will assist the development of functional polymer nanocomposites and help broaden the applications of polymer nanocomposites in general.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by a grant from the Fundamental R&D Program for Technology of World Premier Materials funded by the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy, Republic of Korea. We also acknowledge the financial support from a Korea Institute of Science and Technology internal project. S.-S. Lee appreciates the research grant from the KU-KIST Graduate School.
